learningOutcomes: Define democracy and explain its key features
Describe the structure of India's government (legislature, executive, judiciary)
Explain the role of elections in a democracy
realLifeContexts: How India's general elections work and why every adult gets a vote
Why the Supreme Court can strike down laws passed by Parliament
How local panchayati raj brings democracy to villages
commonMisconceptions: Democracy means majority can do anything they want
The President of India is the most powerful person in government
Elections alone make a country democratic

learningOutcomes: Explain federalism and centre-state relations in India
Describe the functions of the three branches of government
Analyse how constitutional amendments work
Explain the role of political parties and pressure groups
realLifeContexts: Why education is on the Concurrent List and both central and state governments make laws on it
How the 73rd Amendment created panchayati raj institutions
Why coalition governments are more common since 1989
commonMisconceptions: India is a federal country exactly like the USA
State governments have complete independence from the centre
Constitutional amendments require only a simple majority always

learningOutcomes: Evaluate challenges to Indian democracy (corruption, communalism, regionalism)
Analyse electoral reforms and their impact
Compare Indian democracy with other democratic models
Assess the role of civil society and media in democratic accountability
realLifeContexts: How the RTI Act empowers citizens to hold government accountable
Why the Anti-Defection Law was introduced after political instability
How social media has changed political campaigns in India
commonMisconceptions: Judicial activism always strengthens democracy
EVMs are the only cause of electoral disputes
Civil society organizations are politically neutral by nature

learningOutcomes: Describe the establishment of British rule in India
Explain the causes and effects of the 1857 revolt
Identify key figures of the Indian independence movement
realLifeContexts: Why the 1857 revolt is called India's first war of independence by some historians
How the Salt March became a symbol of resistance against British rule
Why partition of India in 1947 created two nations
commonMisconceptions: All Indians uniformly supported the independence movement
The British only exploited India with no administrative contributions
Partition was solely a religious division with no political causes

learningOutcomes: Analyse the economic impact of British colonialism on India
Explain the rise of nationalism and different strands (moderate, extremist, revolutionary)
Describe the role of women and marginalised communities in the freedom struggle
Assess the reasons for and consequences of partition
realLifeContexts: How British textile policies destroyed Indian handloom industry
Why Dadabhai Naoroji's 'drain of wealth' theory resonated with nationalists
How Partition's violence shaped India-Pakistan relations for decades
commonMisconceptions: The moderate nationalists achieved nothing for independence
Partition was inevitable from the start of British rule
Gandhi's non-violence was the only strategy used by freedom fighters

learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate historiographical debates on Indian nationalism
Analyse how caste, class, and gender intersected in the independence movement
Assess the long-term economic and political legacies of colonialism
Evaluate subaltern and nationalist perspectives on Indian history
realLifeContexts: How Ambedkar's perspective on independence differed from Gandhi's
Why tribal communities often had separate resistance movements from mainstream nationalism
How colonial land revenue systems created lasting rural inequality
commonMisconceptions: Subaltern history simply reverses elite history without complexity
Ambedkar and Gandhi were only opponents, never collaborators
Decolonization automatically ended all colonial economic structures

learningOutcomes: Identify major physical features of India (mountains, rivers, plains, coasts)
Describe types of natural resources and their distribution
Explain factors affecting climate in India
realLifeContexts: Why Mumbai gets more rainfall than Delhi (geography of monsoon)
How the Himalayas affect India's climate and protect it from cold winds
Why coal is concentrated in Jharkhand and not Tamil Nadu
commonMisconceptions: India has a uniform tropical climate everywhere
Natural resources are unlimited if managed well
The Himalayas are only significant as tourist destinations

learningOutcomes: Explain the distribution and conservation of forest and wildlife resources
Analyse water resources, scarcity, and management strategies
Describe land use patterns and soil types
Explain mineral and energy resources and their exploitation
realLifeContexts: Why Rajasthan uses traditional baoli stepwells for water conservation
How deforestation in the Western Ghats leads to flooding and landslides
Why India is both energy-poor and renewable energy-rich simultaneously
commonMisconceptions: Dams always solve water scarcity problems
Forest loss only affects wildlife not human communities
Solar energy is already cheaper and more accessible than coal everywhere in India

learningOutcomes: Evaluate sustainable development approaches to resource management
Analyse regional disparities in resource distribution and development
Assess India's vulnerability to climate change impacts
Apply geopolitical analysis to resource conflicts (water sharing, border disputes)
realLifeContexts: How the Cauvery water dispute between Karnataka and Tamil Nadu illustrates inter-state resource conflict
Why small island nations and coastal India face existential risk from sea-level rise
How India's renewable energy goals link to both climate action and energy security
commonMisconceptions: Sustainable development means no industrial growth
Climate change effects are uniform across all regions of India
Water disputes are purely technical problems solvable by engineering alone

learningOutcomes: Define development and explain different goals beyond income
Describe the sectors of the Indian economy
Explain the concept of GDP and per capita income
realLifeContexts: Why Kerala scores high on HDI despite lower per capita income than some states
How MNREGA provides rural employment as a safety net
Why a country can grow its GDP while poverty remains high
commonMisconceptions: Higher GDP always means better development
The primary sector is unimportant in modern India
All employment in India is in the formal sector

learningOutcomes: Explain the role of money and credit in development
Describe globalisation and its impact on Indian economy
Analyse consumer rights and markets
Explain food security and the public distribution system
realLifeContexts: How SHGs helped rural women in Andhra Pradesh access credit without collateral
How MNCs entering India after 1991 affected domestic industries
Why the PDS is essential for food security during famines and droughts
commonMisconceptions: Globalisation has only benefited developing countries
Microfinance always helps the poor without any risks
Food security means only having enough food supply nationally

learningOutcomes: Evaluate India's development trajectory since independence
Analyse inequality, poverty, and policy interventions
Assess the impact of liberalisation on different sectors and groups
Apply development economics frameworks to Indian case studies
realLifeContexts: Why India's demographic dividend may become a demographic disaster without skill development
How the shift from Five Year Plans to NITI Aayog changed India's planning approach
Why India's growth has been called jobless growth in manufacturing
commonMisconceptions: Five Year Plans ended because they failed completely
India's demographic dividend will automatically boost growth
Inclusive growth and high GDP growth are always complementary

learningOutcomes: Describe the causes and effects of World War I and World War II
Explain the rise of nationalism in Europe
Identify key events of the Cold War
realLifeContexts: How the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand triggered WWI
Why the UN was created after WWII to prevent future wars
How the Berlin Wall symbolised Cold War division of the world
commonMisconceptions: WWI was caused only by the assassination in Sarajevo
The Cold War involved direct military battles between USA and USSR
The UN can always prevent wars between countries

learningOutcomes: Analyse the causes of the rise of fascism and Nazism
Explain decolonisation movements in Asia and Africa
Describe the Russian Revolution and its global impact
Assess the economic causes of the Great Depression
realLifeContexts: How Germany's post-WWI economic humiliation created conditions for Hitler's rise
How India's independence in 1947 inspired African decolonisation movements
How the Great Depression led to Keynesian economics and government intervention
commonMisconceptions: Fascism was only an Italian phenomenon
The Russian Revolution was welcomed by all Russians
Decolonisation ended all forms of colonial influence after independence

learningOutcomes: Evaluate historiographical interpretations of major 20th century events
Analyse the role of ideology in shaping the Cold War
Assess post-Cold War world order and new conflicts
Apply historical thinking to understand present-day geopolitical tensions
realLifeContexts: How the Vietnam War was a Cold War proxy conflict with devastating local consequences
Why the 2008 financial crisis was called the worst since the Great Depression
How China's rise is reshaping the post-Cold War US-led world order
commonMisconceptions: The end of the Cold War meant the end of major ideological conflicts
US unipolarity after 1991 was stable and permanent
Historical revisionism means distorting facts rather than reinterpreting evidence

learningOutcomes: Define social inequality and identify its forms in India
Describe caste discrimination and constitutional protections against it
Explain gender inequality and its manifestations
realLifeContexts: Why Dr. Ambedkar fought against caste discrimination and led mass conversions
How the girl child ratio in Haryana reflects gender discrimination at birth
Why child labour persists despite laws against it in India
commonMisconceptions: Caste discrimination no longer exists after the Constitution banned untouchability
Gender inequality only affects girls in rural areas
Reservations are the complete solution to caste discrimination

learningOutcomes: Explain communalism, regionalism, and casteism as challenges to national unity
Analyse poverty, its causes, and government anti-poverty programmes
Describe urbanisation challenges: slums, migration, urban poverty
Assess the role of education in social mobility
realLifeContexts: How Dharavi in Mumbai represents urban poverty despite proximity to India's financial capital
Why inter-state migration increases during drought years
How the Right to Education Act tried to make schooling universal in India
commonMisconceptions: Urban poverty is less severe than rural poverty
Migration from villages to cities always improves migrant welfare
Poverty is only about lack of income and not multidimensional

learningOutcomes: Critically evaluate the effectiveness of reservation policies
Analyse intersectionality of caste, gender, and class in social exclusion
Assess impact of globalisation on social inequalities in India
Evaluate social movements and their role in policy change
realLifeContexts: How the Mandal Commission report led to the OBC reservation expansion and political upheaval
Why Dalit women face triple discrimination based on gender, caste, and class
How the Narmada Bachao Andolan challenged development at the cost of displacement
commonMisconceptions: Reservation policies are unchanged since 1950
Social movements always succeed in changing government policy
Intersectionality is a Western concept with no relevance to India

learningOutcomes: Read and interpret different types of maps
Explain environmental issues: pollution, deforestation, and climate change
Identify major environmental disasters and their causes
realLifeContexts: How Google Maps uses latitude and longitude to locate places
Why Delhi's air quality index matters for public health
How the Bhopal gas tragedy became a landmark in industrial disaster accountability
commonMisconceptions: Maps always show things as they currently are
Climate change and weather are the same phenomenon
Environmental pollution only affects human health, not ecosystems

learningOutcomes: Interpret topographic maps and remote sensing images
Analyse causes and consequences of major environmental problems
Explain international environmental agreements and India's role
Describe disaster management strategies
realLifeContexts: How ISRO uses satellite imagery for flood mapping and disaster response
Why India's position on the Paris Agreement balances development and climate action
How the Kerala floods of 2018 exposed gaps in disaster preparedness
commonMisconceptions: Remote sensing satellites can directly measure temperature without algorithms
The Paris Agreement legally forces countries to meet emission targets
Disaster management only happens after a disaster strikes

learningOutcomes: Apply GIS thinking to spatial analysis of social and environmental problems
Evaluate India's environmental policy and its effectiveness
Analyse climate justice debates between developed and developing countries
Assess urban environmental challenges using case studies
realLifeContexts: Why India argues for per capita emissions as the fair climate metric, not absolute totals
How Urban Heat Island effect makes Indian cities warmer than surrounding rural areas
Why EIA controversies delay major infrastructure projects in India
commonMisconceptions: Climate justice only concerns developing countries not corporations
Smart cities automatically solve urban environmental problems
EIAs are independent assessments free from political pressure in all cases

learningOutcomes: Explain the role and structure of the United Nations
Describe global challenges: terrorism, poverty, climate change
Identify India's position in international affairs
realLifeContexts: Why India seeks a permanent seat on the UN Security Council
How the Non-Aligned Movement was relevant during the Cold War
Why cross-border terrorism is a persistent issue in South Asia
commonMisconceptions: The UN can force any country to obey its resolutions
Non-Aligned Movement means a country has no international alliances at all
Terrorism is only a problem for developing countries

learningOutcomes: Analyse India's relations with neighbours (Pakistan, China, Bangladesh)
Explain the impact of globalisation on politics and culture
Describe refugee crises and human rights issues globally
Assess trade blocs and regional integration (WTO, ASEAN, EU)
realLifeContexts: How India-China border tensions affect trade and strategic relations simultaneously
Why the Rohingya refugee crisis created tensions between Myanmar, Bangladesh, and India
How WTO dispute settlement affects India's agricultural subsidies
commonMisconceptions: India-Pakistan relations are only about Kashmir
Regional integration always reduces national sovereignty
Refugee crises are only caused by wars not economic migration

learningOutcomes: Evaluate realist, liberal, and constructivist theories of international relations
Analyse India's strategic autonomy in a multipolar world
Assess the role of soft power in India's foreign policy
Evaluate global governance gaps in addressing climate, health, and inequality crises
realLifeContexts: How India's Vaccine Maitri initiative used vaccine supply as soft power diplomacy
Why India joined the QUAD while maintaining ties with Russia (strategic autonomy)
How COVID-19 exposed global governance failures in health preparedness
commonMisconceptions: Soft power is less important than hard power in modern diplomacy
India's strategic autonomy means neutrality on all global issues
BRICS is a rival to the UN and Western institutions
